Once features are extracted, they have to be **matched** across frames — finding the same real-world point seen from two different camera positions. Below, the left panel is your live camera and the right panel is a frame from a moment ago; lines link the keypoints ORB matched between them. Move the camera slowly to see the correspondences shift.

```{=html}
<iframe src="pages/html/orb_matching.html" width="100%" height="420px" frameborder="0" scrolling="no" allow="camera"></iframe>
```

#### How it works

Each keypoint carries the 256-bit descriptor built in the previous step. Matching simply asks: for a descriptor in the current frame $F_t$, **which descriptor in an earlier frame $F_{t-1}$ is most similar?**

1. **Nearest-neighbour search using Hamming distance.** Similarity between two binary descriptors is the **Hamming distance** — the number of differing bits (a fast XOR + bit-count); a small distance means the patches look nearly identical. For each descriptor in frame A, find its **nearest neighbour** in frame B — the descriptor with the smallest Hamming distance. That pair is a *probable* match: likely the same scene point seen from the two camera positions (the lines in the demo).
2. **Reject bad matches.** Nearest-neighbour always returns *something*, even when the true point is occluded or out of view, so the candidates must be filtered. Common tests: a **distance threshold** (drop matches that are still too different), the **ratio test** (drop a match if the best and second-best neighbours are nearly tied — it's ambiguous), and a **cross-check** (keep a match only if A→B and B→A agree on each other). This demo uses cross-check plus a distance threshold.

The result is a set of point correspondences between the two frames. They're only *probable* — a final geometric check during **Camera Motion Estimation** (the next step) removes the matches that are inconsistent with a single camera motion.
